back_translation: Jesus departed from there and came to His hometown, and the disciples followed Him as well.

When the Sabbath came, He began to teach in the synagogue, and many people who heard were astonished, saying, 'Where did this man get these things? What is the wisdom that has been given to this man? And how can such great miraculous power performed through his hands be like this?'
'Isn't this man the carpenter? Isn't he the son of Lady Mary, and the older brother of James, Joses, Judas, and Simon? Aren't his younger sisters here with us?' So they took offense at Him.
Then Jesus said to them, 'A prophet is not dishonored, except in his hometown, among his relatives, and in his own household.'
He could not perform any miracle there, except that He laid His royal hand on a few sick people and healed them.
He marveled because of their unbelief. Then He went around teaching in the surrounding villages.
He called the twelve disciples to come, began to send them out two by two, and gave them authority over unclean spirits.
And He charged them not to take anything along on the journey, except only a staff — no bread, no bag, no money in their belts.
But to wear sandals, and not to wear two tunics.
He said to them, 'Wherever you enter into a house, stay there until you depart from that place.
If any place will not welcome you and refuses to listen, when you go out from there, shake off the dust under your feet, as a witness against them.'
So they went out and preached for people to repent.
They drove out many demons and anointed many sick people with oil and healed them.

At that time, King Herod heard about Jesus, because His name had become widely renowned. People were saying, 'John the Baptizer has risen from the dead, and that is why great miraculous powers are at work in him.'
Some said, 'He is Elijah,' and some said, 'He is a prophet, like one of the prophets of former times.'
But when Herod heard this, he said, 'John whom I beheaded has risen from the dead.'
Earlier, Herod himself had ordered that John be seized, bound, and imprisoned, on account of Lady Herodias, the wife of Philip his younger brother, since Herod had married her.
For John had been telling Herod, 'It is not lawful according to the Torah for you to take your brother's wife as your own wife.'
Lady Herodias held a bitter grudge against John and wanted to have him killed, but she could not.
Because Herod feared and revered John, knowing him to be a righteous and holy man, so he protected him; and when he heard John, he was greatly perplexed in heart, yet he gladly listened to him.

When the opportune day came — that is, the anniversary of Herod's birth — Herod prepared a banquet for the nobles, the commanders of a thousand soldiers, and the leading people of the region of Galilee.

When the daughter of Lady Herodias came in and danced, she was pleasing to Herod and the table-companions, and the king said to the young woman, 'Whatever you ask of me, ask, and I will give it to you.'
He swore an oath to her, 'Whatever you ask of me, I will give to you, even up to half of my kingdom.'
So the young woman went out and asked her mother, 'What should this child ask?' Her mother answered, 'Ask for the head of John the Baptizer.'
The young woman immediately hurried back into the king's audience and requested, 'I beg you to graciously give me the head of John the Baptizer on a platter at this very moment.'
The king was extremely grieved in heart, but because of his oaths and the table-companions, he did not wish to refuse her.
Immediately the king sent the executioner, ordering him to bring John's head; so the executioner went and beheaded John in the prison.
And brought John's head on a platter and gave it to the young woman, and the young woman gave it to her own mother.
When John's disciples learned of this, they came to that place and respectfully took his body away and laid it in a tomb.
As for the apostles, they returned and gathered with Jesus, and reported to Him everything they had done and taught.
He said to them, 'You all come away with Me to a solitary place by yourselves and rest a little.' Because there were so many people coming and going that they did not even have time to eat.
So He and His disciples got into the boat and went to a solitary place by themselves.
But many people saw them sailing away and recognized Him, so they ran by land from every town to that place and arrived there before them.
When He had disembarked from the boat, He looked and saw a great crowd, and He had compassion on them, because they were like sheep that have no shepherd. So He began to teach them many things.
When the time had grown late, His disciples came and said to Him, 'This place is solitary, and the time is already late.
Please dismiss them, so they can go to the surrounding countryside and villages and buy food to eat themselves.'
But He answered them, 'You give them food to eat.' The disciples said to Him, 'Are we to go and buy two hundred denarii worth of bread to give them to eat?'
He said to them, 'How many loaves do you have? Go and see.' When they had checked, they said, 'We have five loaves, and two fish.'
He commanded the disciples to have the crowd sit down in groups on the green grass.
So the crowd sat down in groups — some groups of one hundred, some groups of fifty.
He took the five loaves and the two fish, looked up to heaven, gave thanks, broke the loaves, and gave them to the disciples to distribute to the crowd; and He divided the two fish among everyone.
Everyone ate and was filled.
The disciples gathered the leftover broken bread and pieces of fish and filled twelve baskets.
Now the number of those who ate that bread was five thousand men.
Immediately He urged the disciples to get into the boat and go ahead of Him to the town of Bethsaida, while He dismissed the crowd.
When He had bidden them farewell, He went up on the mountain to pray.
When evening came, the boat was in the middle of the sea, and He was alone on the shore.

He saw the disciples toiling at rowing, because the wind was against them. About the fourth watch of that night, He came to them, walking on the surface of the sea-water, and He intended to pass by them.
But when they saw Him walking on the surface of the sea-water, they thought it was a ghost, so they cried out.
For all of them saw Him and were terrified. But immediately He said to them, 'Take courage, it is I, do not be afraid.'
He came up to them in the boat, and the wind became calm. They were utterly astounded in their hearts to the very limit.
Because they did not yet understand the matter of the loaves, but their hearts were stubborn and hardened.
When they had crossed over, they came to the region of Gennesaret and anchored the boat there.
As soon as they got out of the boat, the people recognized Him.
The people ran throughout that whole region and began to carry the sick on stretchers to wherever they heard He was staying.

Wherever He went in — whether village, town, or countryside — the people brought the sick and laid them in the marketplaces, and begged Him just to let them touch the edge of His royal garment, and everyone who touched Him was healed.
